details.text: Derivative: يَعْدِلُونَ is from عَدَلَ – يَعْدِلُ – عَدْل which can mean “to make equal” and is used here to mean “to make equals and partners” to their Rabb (Lord).
Derivative: حَاقَ – يَحِيقُ carries the meanings of to surround, afflict from all sides, take grip of, seize and engulf. Its use depicts punishment and consequences from which there is no escape.
Derivative: اسْتُهْزِئَ is the passive form of اِسْتَهْزَأ – يَسْتَهْزِئُ – اِسْتِهْزَاءً which means to mock or ridicule somebody or something in an insulting and degrading manner.
Derivative: سَخِرُوا is from سَخِرَ – يَسْخَرُ – سُخْرِيَة which means to make fun of, ridicule and deride someone or something.
Derivative: يَصِفُونَ is from وَصَفَ – وَصْف which means to describe something in detail. يَصِفُونَ (Yasifoona) here means “they describe” in terms of ascribing qualities and attributes to Allah.

Derivative: خَرَقُوا from خَرَقَ which means to rip and tear apart, to exceed bounds and norms and to violate and break them. خَرَقُوا here means that “they invented” or “they attributed” whilst implying that by inventing and attributing that they violated all bounds and norms and tore them apart.

Derivative: بَدِيعُ (Badee’) comes from بَدَعَ which means to be creative, original and to come with something that has no precedent before it. بَدِيعُ (Badee’) is the One who creates, initiates and originates without precedent from nothing.
Derivative: تُدْرِكُ is from أَدْرَكَ – يُدْرِكُ – إِدْرَاك which is one of the words in Arabic which means “to reach” and implies reaching something and fully grasping what it is.

Derivative: اللَّطِيفُ (Al-Lateef) comes from لُطْف (Lutf) which carries the meanings of to be delicate and fine. اللَّطِيفُ (Al-Lateef) refers to the One who has precise and subtle power who can execute in the most delicate, fine and precise ways.

Derivative: بَصَائِرُ (Basaa’ir) is the plural of بَصِيرَة (Baseerah) which refers to something that you can see and fully understand. Here it is used in reference to the clear signs and revelations (Aayaat).

Derivative: دَرَسْتَ is from دَرَسَ which means to study but in old Arabic it implies the meaning of to learn from somebody else and to have someone else read to you i.e., provide you with the information.
Derivative: يَسُبُّوا is from سَبَّ – يَسُبُّ – سَبٌّ means to insult someone with offensive language to degrade someone or something.

Derivative: أَيْمَانِ (Aymaan) is the plural of يَمِين (Yameen) which in origin refers to the “right hand” and is used to mean “an oath” in the case where the right hand was symbolic in relation to swearing an oath. The right hand is also a symbol of honour and dignity attached to the oath and by swearing an oath by the right hand one would be placing their honour and dignity at stake if the oath was to be broken.
Derivative: جَهْدَ comes from جَهَدَ which means to put oneself out and take pains. Here in relation to swearing their oaths it implies that they go out of their way to swear their strongest oaths.

Derivative: يَعْمَهُونَ comes from عَمِهَ (‘Amiha) which has been said to mean to “be blind” but in relation to one’s heart, unlike عَمِيَ (‘Amiyah) which refers to being bling in terms of one’s sight. It has also been said mean “to wander and stumble around in a state of bewilderment and being directionless”. Theses meanings can be combined in thePhrase: ‘to wander (around) blindly”.
Derivative: غُرُورًا (Ghurooran) means as an act of deception. غُرُور (Ghuroor) is when you deceive and trick somebody taking advantage of their heedlessness or unawareness.

Derivative: زُخْرُفَ (Zukhruf) comes from زَخْرَفَ which means to adorn and decorate something to make it look nice. زُخْرُفَ (Zukhruf) refers to the decoration, embellishment or adornment. Here in conjunction with الْقَوْلِ in the statement زُخْرُفَ الْقَوْلِ it means speech that has been beautified and embellished i.e., to make it attractive.
Derivative: تَصْغَىٰ comes from صَغِيَ – يَصْغَى and it means to incline and bend towards (إِلَى - Ilaa) something due to an attachment, affection or obsession towards it.

Derivative: يَقْتَرِفُوا is from اِقْتَرَفَ – يَقْتَرِفُ – اِقْتِرَافًا which means “to earn, acquire or gain” something and is used when you earn or gain via illegal means. Due to this it has also taken on the meaning of “to perpetrate” or “commit” a crime or sin.

Derivative: يَخْرُصُونَ – is from خَرَصَ – يَخْرِصُ – خَرْص which means to guess and conjecture. It refers to when someone makes an estimate, but it is not based on proof. From this the word خَرَّاص (Kharraas) is derived which refers to someone who just makes things up based purely on conjecture.
Derivative: الْمُهْتَدِينَ is the plural of المُهْتَدي (Al-Muhtadiy) which is from the verb اِهْتَدَى and refers to the one who is committed to following guidance.
Derivative: صَغَارٌ (Saghaar) which comes from صَغُرَ which means “to be or become small”. صَغَارٌ (Saghaar) refers to the state of being made small and brought down i.e., lowly and humiliated.
Derivative: ضَيِّقًا (Dayyiq) means “constricted” like when one is in a tight space.

Derivative: حَرَجًا (Haraj) refers to a great discomfort and difficulty felt due to restrictions being placed on you, being confined and feeling cornered. It refers to an intense constriction which is greater than ضِيق (Deeq).
Derivative: الرِّجْسَ (Ar-Rijs) refers to anything that the Deen has specified to be filthy. It has also been said to refer to a curse and punishment.
Derivative: يَصَّعَّدُ is originally from تَصَعَّدَ – يتَصَعَّدُ – تَصَعُّد but the تاء (Taa’) has been replaced by a صاد (Saad). تَصَعَّدَ means to ascend and climb up.
- The expansion (شَرْح -Sharh) of the chest (الصَّدْر – As-Sadr) is an expression to mean that the person is prepared for the truth to settle and find a home within him.

Derivative: مَثْوَى (Mathwaa) comes from ثَوَى – يَثْوِي which means to reside in a place and settle there. مَثْوَى (Mathwaa) is the place that you reside and settle in and implies that it is for a long time.
Derivative: الْغَنِيُّ (Al-Ghaniy) is the One who has no need. He is rich beyond need and completely self-sufficient.

Derivative: مَّعْرُوشَاتٍ (Ma’rooshaat) comes from عَرَشَ – يَعْرِشُ which means to erect a trellis which is a wooden frame for plants to grow upon like grape vines. مَعْرُوشَة (Ma’rooshah) means “trellised”. It is also said to refer to that which is grown upon and above the ground and spread upon it like watermelons, pumpkins and grape vines.

Derivative: فَرْشًا (Farsh) refers linguistically to that which is spread upon the ground and here it is being used to refer to the livestock or cattle which are close to the ground like sheep. They contrast with the animals which can be used for carrying loads which are referred here to as حَمُولَةً (Hamoolah).
Derivative: الْبَالِغَةُ (Al-Baalighah) means that which reaches far i.e., hard hitting, penetrating and conclusive.
Derivative: الْحُجَّةُ (Al-Hujjah) refers to a clear and irrefutable argument and proof.
Derivative: صَدَفَ means to turn away from something and to shun it.
Derivative: شِيَعًا (Shiya’) is the plural of شِيعَة (Shee’ah) which literally means “a faction”.

- “Those who (الَّذِينَ) separated or divided (فَرَّقُوا) their Deen (دِينَهُمْ)” – This can refer to separating some parts of the Deen from others or dividing it into parts or it can refer to making differences amongst themselves in respect to that which is clear and what they should have been unified upon.

Derivative: يُصْرَفْ is from صَرَفَ and with عَن (‘An) it means to be diverted and turned away from something. In this context it implies to be spared and saved from the punishment on the Day of Judgement.

- “And he was not (وَمَا كَانَ) from the Mushrikeen (مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ)” – The use of “مَا كَانَ” implies that “he was never from the polytheists” as it is a form of negation that completely precludes the possibility.

Derivative: مَحْيَا (Mahyaa) is a مَصْدَر مِيمِي (Masdar Meemiy) that means the same as the original Masdar of حَيَاة (Hayaat) which means “life” in reference to the life that is lived until one reaches death. مَحْيَا (Mahyaa) also takes the same form as the adverb of time and place (ظَرْف المكان والزَّمان) and can therefore also linguistically mean the place and time of life.

Derivative: مَمَاتِ (Mamaat) is a مَصْدَر مِيمِي (Masdar Meemiy) that means the same as the original Masdar of مَوْت (Mawt) which means “Death”. مَمَاتِ (Mamaat) also takes the same form as the adverb of time and place (ظَرْف المكان والزَّمان) and can therefore also linguistically mean the place and time of death.

قُلْ إِنَّ صَلَاتِي وَنُسُكِي وَمَحْيَايَ وَمَمَاتِي لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ

- “Say (قُلْ) verily (إِنَّ) my prayer (صَلَاتِي) and my sacrifices (وَنُسُكِي) and my life and my death (وَمَحْيَايَ وَمَمَاتِي) are for Allah (لِلَّهِ) the Rabb of the worlds (رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ)”. The statement: “And my life and my death (وَمَحْيَايَ وَمَمَاتِي)” implies: What I undertake in this life in terms of actions and what I die upon in terms of Iman (belief) and righteous acts.

Derivative: تَخْتَلِفُونَ is from اِخْتَلَفَ – اِخْتِلافًا which means to differ and disagree. It is used in Quranic language to refer to the اِخْتِلاف (Ikhtilaaf i.e., disagreement and differing) in respect to the fundamentals of the Deen and not the branches. It is this type of اِخْتِلاف (Ikhtilaaf i.e., disagreement and differing) in relation to the fundamentals of the Deen that leads to division (تَفَرُّق – Tafarruq) amongst the followers of a Deen into factions.
Derivative: خَلَائِفَ (Khalaa’if) is a plural of خَلِيفَة (Khaleefah) and refers to those who succeed those who proceeded them i.e., successors.
Derivative: الْعِقَابِ (Al-‘Iqaab) refers to the punishment that comes as a consequence of something.
Derivative: الْقَاهِرُ (Al-Qaahir) refers to the One who is overpowering and indomitable.
Derivative: طِينٍ (Teen) refers to soil that has been exposed to water or moisture, or clay.

Derivative: تَمْتَرُونَ is from اِمْتَرَى – يَمْتَرِي – اِمْتِراءً which in origin comes from the word مِرْيَة (Miryah) and the root letters are م ر ي and is one of the words used in the Qur’an to refer to “having doubt”. مِرْيَة (Miryah) refers to a particular type of doubt. It is doubt in something that is a known fact and is undisputed. The type of doubt that is insincere and is used for the sake of making an argument and causing confusion.
Derivative: مُّسَمًّى (Musammaa) means “to be named” and can imply that the thing it is describing has been “well named” or “named with various names”.
Derivative: أَجَلٌ (Ajal) refers to the period of time that starts from the present or now and ends at the specified appointment i.e., the deadline.

هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُم مِّن طِينٍ ثُمَّ قَضَىٰ أَجَلًا ۖ وَأَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى عِندَهُ

- Two أََجَل (Ajals) are mentioned here. The first applies to the lifespan of the human i.e., a term that comes to an end at death after having lived for a specified time. And the second is from the time of death until being raised on the Day of resurrection.
Derivative: افْتَرَىٰ – اِفْتِراء means to make something up, to invent and fabricate it. It is something that is made up out of thin air without any basis or evidence for it.

Derivative: نَحْشُرُ comes from حَشَرَ – حَشْر which means to “gather up or together”. It differs from جَمَعَ – جَمْع (Jam’) because Jam’a implies the gathering that is from the peoples’ own accord or volition whilst Hashr implies a compelled gathering like when animals are herded and made to gather up.

Derivative: تَزْعُمُونَ comes from زَعَمَ which is said to refer to the act of assuming something based on full confidence without knowledge. Others however have said that it refers to an assertion or claim that is made concerning something in which there is doubt and cannot be ascertained. It is most commonly used and particularly in the Qur’an to refer to a claim or assertion that is false in origin. It can also refer to a statement, claim and assertion that could be true or false but cannot be ascertained either way.
Derivative: أَكِنَّةً (Akinnah) is the plural of كِنَان (Kinaan) which refers to a cover that protects something from penetrating beyond it.
Derivative: وَقْرًا (Waqr) refers to a heaviness which in respect to the ears means a deafness.
Derivative: أَسَاطِيرُ (Asaateer) is the plural of أُسْطُورَة (Ustoorah) an refers to legends, fables and myths i.e., just stories that are told and passed from generation to generation.
Derivative: يَنْأَوْنَ is from نَأَى – يَنْأَى – نَأْيًا and it means to be distant from (عَن) something.

Derivative: وُقِفُوا is the passive form from وَقَفَ which means to stop and to stand. It can mean both to stand and to be made to stand or make stand as وََقَفَ (Waqafa) also held the meaning of أَوْقَفَ (Awqafa) in classical Arabic: وُقِفُوا means “They are made to stand”.

Derivative: فَرَّطْنَا comes from فَرَّطَ – يُفَرِّطُ – تَفْرِيط which means to fall short in something and neglect it by not giving it its rightful due. In this regard it means that we did not give it the care and attention that it deserved in reference to the final hour.

Derivative: أَوْزَارَ (Awzaar) is the plural of وِزْر (Wizr) which refers to a heavy load or responsibility that is carried and here it implies burdens and sins. It comes from وَزَرَ – يَزِرُ which means to bear or carry a burden, responsibility or weight.

Derivative: يَا حَسْرَتَنَا is and expression used to express an emotional state related to the severity or intenseness of حَسْرَة (Hasrah) that is being felt in the case where حَسْرَة (Hasrah) refers to “Regret, grief and loss”.

Derivative: مُعْرِضِينَ comes from أَعْرَضَ – يُعْرِضُ – إِعْرَاض which means to not give something any regard nor concern, like ignoring it. It can also imply turning away from something and having an aversion to it.

Derivative: يَتَضَرَّعُونَ is from تَضَرَّعَ means to make oneself so humble and submissive before something as if you are the lowest of things (before it). It has been said that تَضَرَّعَ combines the meanings of خُشُوع (Khushoo’) and خُضُوع (Khudoo’) which means to be in a state of humility (i.e., being humbled) and subservience.
Derivative: مُّبْلِسُونَ comes from أَبْلَسَ – إِبْلاس which means to lose all hope of anything good coming in the future i.e., to be in a state of abject despair.
- قَطْع دَابِر شَيْءٍ - “To severe or cut (قَطْع) the Daabir (دَابِر - The end) of something (شَيْءٍ)” is an Arabic expression to mean to eradicate something and completely uproot it.

Derivative: نُصَرِّفُ is from صَرَّفَ – يُصَرِّفُ – تَصْرِيف and it means to alternate between things and to diversify. In respect to the Aayaat, it means to provide various different ways of presenting them.
- “Have (أَ) you seen (رَأَيْتُمْ)” – رَأَى means “to see” and is also used to mean “to consider and think about” something i.e., Have you considered or thought about?
Derivative: تَتَفَكَّرُونَ – فَكَّرَ – تَفْكِير means “to think” whilst تَفَكَّرَ – تَفَكُّر means to “think deeply about something”.

Derivative: نُفَصِّلُ is from فَصَّلَ – يُفَصِّلُ – تَفْصِيل which carries the meaning of separating, dividing and breaking things up into smaller parts. It is used to mean to explain and present in detail to make something clear.
Derivative: بَيِّنَةٍ (Bayyinah) refers to a clear proof and the decisive proof between truth and falsehood.
Derivative: الْفَاصِلِينَ is the plural of الفَاصِل (Al-Faasil) and refers to those who pass or possess the final and decisive judgement or decision.

Derivative: مِّدْرَارًا (Midraar) comes from دَرَّ – دَرًّا which alongside its derivatives refers to abundance of flowing. مِّدْرَارًا (Midraar) is used to describe abundant rainfall when used in conjunction with the sky or clouds.
Derivative: أَنشَأْنَا comes from أَنْشَأَ – يُنْشِئُ – إِنْشاء which means to make or cause something to rise and allow it to grow.
Derivative: كَرْبٍ (Karb) refers to a very difficult, distressing and overburdening situation.
Derivative: مُسْتَقَرٌّ (Mustaqarr) refers to a time or place where something is settled. Here it refers to a time.

Derivative: يَخُوضُونَ – is from خَاضَ – يَخُوضُ – خَوْضًا which in origin means to plunge into something and implies getting into something hastily and without thinking about it. When related to speech means to involve oneself in speaking about things with a lack of seriousness, thought and respect i.e., fruitless speech, just talking and arguing for the sake of it. It can include talking about something to ridicule it or waste time which is engaged in for the purpose of play and entertainment.

Derivative: لَمَسُوا is from لَمَسَ – يَلْمِسُ – لَمْس and refers to physically touching and feeling something with ones hands and implies examination i.e., touching and feeling to understand the thing.
Derivative: تَعْدِلْ is from عَدَلَ – عَدْل and instead of referring to justice it has another meaning which is “to ransom” like فَدَى – يَفْدِي – فِدَاء.

Derivative: اسْتَهْوَتْ is from أِسْتَهْوَى – يَسْتِهْوِي – اِسْتِهْوَاء which means to entice and make appealing to the desires. When it is used with Shaytan it implies that the person proceeds just as Shaytan wishes him to as if he has become overcome by him.

- بِالْحَقِّ (Bil-Haqq) can mean “in truth” i.e., as a certain fact, and it can also mean “with purpose”. The opposite of حَقٌَّ (Haqq) is بَاطِل (Baatil) and just as it means “falsehood” it also implies the meaning of “purposelessness” like His statement: وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَاطِلًا “And We did not create the heavens and the earth and what is between them without purpose (بَاطِلًا - Baatilan)”.
Derivative: أَفَلَ – يَأْفُلُ – أَفْلًا means to go down, set or disappear and is used for celestial bodies.
Derivative: بَازِغًا (Baazigh) is from بَزَغَ – يَبْزُغُ – بُزُوغ which means to rise, emerge and break forth like when light breaks forth from darkness. It is used in relation to celestial bodies.

فَلَمَّا رَأَى الشَّمْسَ بَازِغَةً قَالَ هَٰذَا رَبِّي هَٰذَا أَكْبَرُ

- “Then when he saw the sun rising, he said: “This (هَٰذَا) is my Rabb. This (هَٰذَا) is Akbar (Greater)” – The use of هَٰذَا (Haadhaa) which is masculine for “this” here instead of هَٰذِهِ (Haadhihi) which is the feminine of “this” when the apparent reference is الشَّمْسَ (Ash-Shams) which is feminine, can imply that تَوْرِيَة (Tawriyah – Concealment and dissimulation) is being employed. This is when you say something that is understood by those listening in one way whilst its true meaning something else. As such when Ibrahim (as) stated هَٰذَا رَبِّي “This is my Rabb” he meant Allah whilst intending that everybody listening would assume that he was referring to the sun.
Derivative: الْأَمْنِ (Al-Amn) – It refers to feeling safe and secure. It is a peace where you feel secure and safe internally and externally.
Derivative: سُلْطَانًا (Sultaan) implies the meaning of “an authority” and is used to refer to a proof that possesses authority.
Derivative: يَلْبِسُوا comes from لَبَسَ – يَلْبِسُ – لِبْس which can mean to wear and put on (i.e., clothing. It can also imply the meaning of “to mix” which is its intended meaning here.
Derivative: حَبِطَ means to be invalidated, to be wasted and for the fruits of an action to be nullified and become worthless.

Derivative: الْحُكْمَ (Al-Hukm) can refer to sound decision-making ability and judgement. And it can also mean الحِكْمَة (Al-Hikmah) which is “wisdom” and can imply knowledge of the rulings of the revealed Book.

Derivative: اِقْتَدِ is the command form from اِقْتَدَى – يَقْتَدِي – اِقتِدَاء which originates from the root letters of (ق – د - و) from which the word قُدْوَة (Qudwah) is derived which refers to a model or example. اِقْتَدِه (Iqtadih) means to follow and emulate somebody as a model, example or to follow their guidance. The ه (Haa’) at the end is not part of the word from its root but has been added for the purpose of adding emphasis. مُقْتَدَى (Muqtadaa) is said in respect to a religious leader who is being followed whilst the follower is known as a مُقْتَدِي (Muqtadi).

Derivative: ذَرْ is the command form from وَذَرَ – يَذَرُ which is only used in the present and command forms. It means to let or leave something or somebody be or alone which is said in relation to that which is not worth being given attention.

Derivative: مُبَارَكٌ comes from بَارَكَ – يُبَارِكُ – بِرَكَة which means to increase in goodness and full of the power of blessing. مُبَارَكٌ (Mubaarak) means to be increased in goodness and containing many benefits.
- أُمَّ الْقُرَىٰ (Umm ul-Quraa) means “the mother of towns” which refers to the main city or centre in a region and is used like “capital city” is used today. It refers here to Makkah.

Derivative: افْتَرَىٰ in origin comes from the word فِرْيَة (Firyah) which refers to a “lie” or “falsity”. افْتَرَىٰ – يَفْتَرِي – اِفْتِرَاء means to make something up with malicious intent like slander or calumny.

- غَمَرَاتِ الْمَوْتِ refers to the severe and difficult pains and agonies (غَمَرَاتِ - Ghamaraat) connected to death (الْمَوْتِ -Al-Mawt) which could include going in and out of consciousness. It is used to describe the moment when death comes.

Derivative: خَوَّلْنَا is from خَوَّلَ – يُخَوِّلُ – تَخْوِيل which means to give, grant ownership and bestow in terms of wealth, children and other things. It is close to the meaning of أَنْعَمَ (An’ama) which means to give a blessing and تَخْوِيلًا (Takhweel) can mean to envelop somebody in a blessing and be used to refer to those blessings that your life is wrapped up in and surrounded by.
Derivative: تَّقَطَّعَ is more emphatic than قَطَعَ and means to completely severed, cut off and to chop or hack into pieces.

Derivative: فَالِقُ is the active participle (اِسْمُ الفاعِل – Ism ul-Faa’il) from فَلَقَ – يَفْلِقُ – فَلْق which means to split or cleave apart or to tear asunder. It is often used in respect to daybreak i.e., when the light of the sun breaks through.

Derivative: تُؤْفَكُونَ which is the passive form from the verb أَفَكَ – يَأْفِكُ – أَفك which means to divert or turn away. تُؤْفَكُونَ means “you have been diverted or turned away” i.e., from the truth.

إِنَّ اللَّهَ فَالِقُ الْحَبِّ وَالنَّوَىٰ ۖ يُخْرِجُ الْحَيَّ مِنَ الْمَيِّتِ وَمُخْرِجُ الْمَيِّتِ مِنَ الْحَيِّ

- In respect to “bringing” the living (الْحَيَّ – Al-Hayy) from the dead (الْمَيِّتِ Al-Mayyit) Allah uses the verb يُخْرِجُ (Yukhriju – He brings) and when referring to “bringing” the dead (الْمَيِّتِ Al-Mayyit) from the living (الْحَيَّ – Al-Hayy) Allah uses the noun مُخْرِجُ (Mukhrij - Brings). The verb in Arabic is associated with movement whilst the noun is associated with stillness and constancy. As such the verb is used with that which is living whilst the noun is used here with that which is dead.

Derivative: مُسْتَوْدَعٌ (Mustawda’) comes from اِسْتَوْدَعَ – يَسْتَوْدِعُ – اِسْتِيدَاع which means to deposit and leave something in safekeeping. مُسْتَوْدَعٌ (Mustawda’) is the safe place that you deposit or store something in.
Derivative: مُسْتَقَرٌّ (Mustaqarr) is from اِسْتَقَرَّ – يَسْتَقِرُّ – اِسْتِقْرار which means to settle in a place. مُسْتَقَرٌّ (Mustaqarr) is the place that someone settles in.

Derivative: طَلْعِ (Tal’) comes from طَلَعَ – يَطْلُعُ – طُلُوع which means to rise up and to come into view. In respect to a date-palm tree it refers to the first of what sprouts out from it in relation to the its fruit.

Derivative: قِنْوَانٌ (Qinwaan) refers to the clusters of dates attached to the branches of date-palms. قِنْوَانٌ (Qinwaan) is used specifically for dates and grapes to describe their bunches and clusters.
Derivative: يَنْعِ (Yan’) means “ripe” or “ripening” and is from يَنَعَ which means “to ripen”.
